skills.skill: Cross-sections and rotations - identify the shapes of two-dimensional cross-sections of prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, and spheres and identify three-dimensional objects generated by rotations of two-dimensional shapes;
skills.prompt: This topic covers cross-sections and rotations (identify the shapes of two-dimensional cross-sections of prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, and spheres and identify three-dimensional objects generated by rotations of two-dimensional shapes;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.10(A)). Rigid motions (translations, reflections, rotations) preserve distance and angle measure, making pre-image and image congruent. Rotations turn figures about a point: 90 CCW (-y,x), 180 (-x,-y), 270 CCW (y,-x). Cross-sections are 2D shapes formed when a plane intersects a 3D figure. The shape depends on the angle and position of the cut. Key vocabulary includes: cone, coordinate notation, cross-section, cylinder, image, pre-image, prism, pyramid, rigid motion, solid of revolution, sphere, transformation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

The shape depends on the angle and position of the cut. Key vocabulary includes: cone, coordinate notation, cross-section, cylinder, image, pre-image, prism, pyramid, rigid motion, solid of revolution, sphere, transformation. skills.skill: Dimensional changes - determine and describe how changes in the linear dimensions of a shape affect its perimeter, area, surface area, or volume, including proportional and non-proportional dimensional change.
skills.prompt: This topic covers dimensional changes (determine and describe how changes in the linear dimensions of a shape affect its perimeter, area, surface area, or volume, including proportional and non-proportional dimensional change.) for Geometry students (TEKS G.10(B)). Surface area formulas: prism SA = 2B + Ph, cylinder SA = 2*pi*r^2 + 2*pi*r*h, cone SA = pi*r^2 + pi*r*l, sphere SA = 4*pi*r^2. Volume formulas: prism V = Bh, pyramid V = (1/3)Bh, cylinder V = pi*r^2*h, cone V = (1/3)*pi*r^2*h, sphere V = (4/3)*pi*r^3. When linear dimensions are multiplied by factor k: perimeter scales by k, area scales by k^2, volume scales by k^3. Key vocabulary includes: area, cone, cylinder, lateral area, net, perimeter, prism, proportional, pyramid, scale factor, similar, sphere, surface area, total surface area, volume. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Dimensional changes - Perimeters of similar figures
skills.prompt: This topic covers dimensional changes (perimeters of similar figures) for Geometry students (TEKS G.10(B)). Similar figures have the same shape with proportional sides and congruent corresponding angles; AA criterion proves triangle similarity. When linear dimensions are multiplied by factor k: perimeter scales by k, area scales by k^2, volume scales by k^3. Key vocabulary includes: AA criterion, area, dilation, perimeter, proportional, scale factor, similar, volume. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Area of regular polygons - apply the formula for the area of regular polygons to solve problems using appropriate units of measure;
skills.prompt: This topic covers area of regular polygons (apply the formula for the area of regular polygons to solve problems using appropriate units of measure;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.11(A)). Area of regular polygon = (1/2)*apothem*perimeter. The apothem is the perpendicular distance from center to a side. Key vocabulary includes: apothem, area, perimeter, regular polygon. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Area of composite figures - determine the area of composite two-dimensional figures comprised of a combination of triangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, kites, regular polygons, or sectors of circles to solve problems using appropriate units of measure;
skills.prompt: This topic covers area of composite figures (determine the area of composite two-dimensional figures comprised of a combination of triangles, parallelograms, trapezoids, kites, regular polygons, or sectors of circles to solve problems using appropriate units of measure;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.11(B)). Sector area = (central angle/360)*pi*r^2, proportional to the central angle. Quadrilateral hierarchy: parallelogram (opposite sides parallel), rectangle (right angles), rhombus (all sides equal), square (both). Properties include diagonal relationships and angle measures. Area of regular polygon = (1/2)*apothem*perimeter. The apothem is the perpendicular distance from center to a side. Area of composite figures: decompose into basic shapes (triangles, rectangles, circles), find each area, and add or subtract. Permutations (nPr) count arrangements where order matters; combinations (nCr) count selections where order does not matter. Key vocabulary includes: apothem, arc, area, center, chord, circle, combination, composite figure, counting principle, decompose, diagonal, factorial, nCr, nPr, parallelogram, perimeter, permutation, quadrilateral, radius, rectangle, regular polygon, rhombus, secant, square, subtract, tangent. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.
